subject: Economy
question: One of the following statements regarding the functions of ‘Reserve Bank of India is incorrect?

options.a: It is mandated with the Right to issue bank notes as one of the key central banking functions.
options.b: It is the right and obligation of RBI to accept monies on account of the Central Government of India.
options.c: It establishes Monetary Policy Committee with an intent to stabilise money supply in the market.
options.d: It can authorize any person to be known as authorized person to deal in foreign exchange or in foreign securities.

explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Statement a is correct. Section 22 of the RBI Act confers the RBI with the sole right to issue bank
notes in India. The issue of bank notes shall be conducted by a department called the Issue
Department, which shall be separated and kept wholly distinct from the Banking Department.
Right to issue bank notes is one of the key central banking functions the RBI is mandated to do so.
Statement b is correct. Section 18 of the RBI Act facilitates the RBI to act as a ‘Lender of Last
Resort’. These provisions of the RBI Act enable the RBI to act as banker to Central Government
and State Governments. Under Sections 20 and 21 of the RBI Act, the RBI shall have an obligation
and right respectively to accept monies for account of the Central Government and to make
payments up to the amount standing to the credit of its account, and to carry out its exchange,
remittance and other banking operations, including the management of the public debt of the
Union.
Statement c is incorrect. Chapter III-F of the RBI Act provides for a statutory basis for the
Monetary Policy Framework and the Monetary Policy Committee. The Central Government, in
consultation with the RBI shall determine the inflation target in terms of the Consumer Price
Index, once in every five years, which needs to be notified in the Official Gazette. Similarly, it is
the Central Government that should constitute Monetary Policy Committee by notification in
the Official Gazette.
Statement d is correct. The powers and responsibilities with respect to external trades and
payments, development and maintenance of foreign exchange market in India are conferred on
the RBI under the provisions of the Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999 (‘FEMA’).

subject: Economy
question: With reference to the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), consider the following statements: 1. All Indian banks require a licence from the RBI for opening a new branch anywhere in India. 2. RBI manages the market borrowing programme of the state governments in India. 3. The interest rates on all categories of deposits and loans of banks are solely determined by the RBI. 4. The Central and the state Governments are required to maintain a minimum cash balance with the RBI. How many of the above given statements are correct?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer
RBI was established under RBI act in 1935 as a private bank. It was converted into central bank
after nationalisation in 1949.
Statement 1 is incorrect: For commencing banking operations in India, whether by an Indian or a
foreign bank, a licence from the Reserve Bank is required. The opening of new branches by banks
and change in the location of existing branches are also regulated as per the Branch Authorisation
Policy. This policy has recently been liberalised significantly and Indian banks no longer require
a licence from the Reserve Bank for opening a branch at a place with population of below
50,000.
Statement 2 is correct: RBI acts as the banker for government. As the debt manager of the state
governments, the mandate of the Reserve Bank is to manage the market borrowing programme
of the state governments.
Statement 3 is incorrect: The interest rates on most of the categories of deposits and lending
transactions have been deregulated and are largely determined by banks. However, the Reserve
Bank regulates the interest rates on savings bank accounts and deposits of non-resident Indians
(NRI), small loans up to rupees two lakh, export credits and a few other categories of advances.
Statement 4 is correct: All the State Governments are required to maintain a minimum cash
balance with the Reserve Bank, which varies from state to state depending on the relative size of
the state budget and economic activity. Under the administrative arrangements, the Central
Government is required to maintain a minimum cash balance with the Reserve Bank.
Important Tips
The function of management of public debt is done by the Internal Debt Management
Department (IDMof the Reserve Bank. IDMD of RBI manages the Market Borrowing
Programme (MBP). This involves issue and servicing aspects i.e., retirement of rupee loans,
interest payment on the loan and handling operational issues.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements: 1. Bonds issued by the Government are commonly called Gilt-Edged Bonds/ securities. 2. Bonds can be issued by governments as well as private companies. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct: G-Secs are also known as gilts or gilt-edged securities.
Statement 2 is correct. Bonds could be issued by governments as well as private companies.
Important Tips
“G-Sec” means a security created and issued by the Government for the purpose of raising a
public loan or for any other purpose as may be notified by the Government.

subject: Economy
question: Which among the following is the correct description of the term, ‘Seigniorage’?

options.a: The cost of metal incurred by a government when it issues new coins.
options.b: The cost incurred by a government when it issues currency.
options.c: The loss made by a government due to soiling and mutilation of the currency in circulation.
options.d: The profit made by a government when it issues currency.

explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Seigniorage refers to the profit made by a government when it issues currency. It is simply the
difference in the value of the currency versus the cost of producing it. Seigniorage is a way for
governments who mint their currency to make an economic profit. The cost of producing
currency is generally lower than the face value of the currency itself. Monetary seigniorage can
be used as an effective monetary policy tool. It can result in debt monetization. Debt
monetization is when a central bank will buy interest-bearing debt with non-interest-bearing
money. It can be a useful tool to control the debt level of an economy.

subject: Economy
question: It is the ratio of the stock of money to the stock of high-powered money in an economy. It refers to how an initial deposit can lead to a bigger final increase in the total money supply in the economy. The concept mentioned here is:
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Money Multiplier (MM) is the ratio of the stock of money to the stock of high-powered money in
an economy. It refers to how an initial deposit can lead to a bigger final increase in the total
money supply in the economy. The multiplier effect refers to the proportional amount of increase,
or decrease, in final income that results from an injection, or withdrawal, of spending.

subject: Economy
question: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) acts as a bankers’ bank. This would imply which of the following? 1. Other bank retains their deposits with the RBI. 2. The RBI lends funds to the commercial banks in times of need. 3. The RBI advises the commercial banks on monetary matters. Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
As Banker to Banks, the Reserve Bank focuses on:
(i) Enabling smooth, swift and seamless clearing and settlement of inter-bank obligations.

(ii) Providing an efficient means of funds transfer for banks.
(iii) Enabling banks to maintain their accounts with the Reserve Bank for statutory reserve
requirements and maintenance of transaction balances.
(iv) Acting as a lender of last resort.

subject: Economy
question: Arrange the following in chronological order: 1. Nationalization of 14 major banks 2. Nationalization of SBI 3. Nationalization of RBI 4. Establishment of RBI Codes:
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
• Nationalization of RBI: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) was established in 1935 as a private bank
under the RBI Act, 1934. It was nationalized in 1949 under the RBI (Transfer of Public Ownership)
Act, 1948.
• Nationalization of SBI: The State Bank of India (SBI) was established in 1955 as a successor to
the Imperial Bank of India, which was a private bank formed in 1921 by merging three presidency
banks. The SBI was nationalized in 1955 under the SBI Act, 1955.
• Nationalization of LIC: The Life Insurance Corporation of India (LIC) was established in 1956 as
a state-owned corporation under the LIC Act, 1956. The LIC was formed by nationalizing 245
private life insurance companies and consolidating them into one entity
• Nationalization of 14 major banks: The first phase of nationalization of banks took place in 1969
under the leadership of Prime Minister Indira Gandhi. Fourteen major private commercial banks
were nationalized under the Banking Companies (Acquisition and Transfer of Undertakings)
Ordinance, 1969.

subject: Economy
question: Which one of the following is not a function of Reserve Bank of India?

options.b: As apex body of Scheduled Commercial Banks

explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Credit creation is not a function of Reserve Bank of India because credit creation is the process
by which commercial banks create money by lending to their customers and generating deposits.
The Reserve Bank of India does not create credit directly, but it regulates the credit creation by
commercial banks through its monetary policy tools such as repo rate, reverse repo rate, cash
reserve ratio, statutory liquidity ratio, etc. The Reserve Bank of India performs the functions of

credit control, apex body of scheduled commercial banks and formulation of monetary policy,
among others.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following statements is not correct regarding the Reserve Bank of India?

options.a: It issues all types of currency notes.
options.b: It functions as a branch bank of the Government of India.

explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Reserve Bank has the sole right to issue banknotes in India. Section 25 of Reserve Bank of India
Act states that the design, form and material of bank notes shall be such as may be approved by
the Central Government after consideration of the recommendations made by the Central Board
of RBI. Indian government is solely responsible for minting coins. ₹1 notes are issued by the
Government of India from time to time and such notes including those issued in the past also
continue to be legal tender for transactions. The coins issued by Government of India under
Section 6 of The Coinage Act, 2011.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements: 1. The Governor of the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is appointed by the Central Government. 2. Certain Provisions in the Constitution of India give the Central Government the right to Issue directions to the RBI in public interest. 3. The Governor of the RBI draws his power from the RBI Act. Which of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct. Section 8(1)(a) of the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934, provides that there
shall be one Governor and not more than four Deputy Governors to be appointed by the central
government on the central board of RBI.
Statement 2 is incorrect. Section 7 of the RBI Act provides that Central Government may from
time to time give such directions to the Bank as it may, after consultation with the Governor of
the Bank, consider necessary in the public interest. Constitution of India does not provide any
provision for Central Government the right to issue directions to the RBI in Public interest.
Statement 3 is correct. According to RBI Act, the Governor shall have powers of general
superintendence and direction of the affairs and the business of the RBI. He/she may exercise
all powers and do all acts and things which may be exercised or done by the RBI.

subject: Economy
question: In India ‘Currency Notes issue system’ is based on:
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
The current system of Indian government to issue notes is “Minimum Reserve System”. Under
this policy, the minimum reserves to be maintained in the form of gold and foreign exchange
should consist of rupees 200 crore. Out this reserve, the value of gold to be maintained is rupees
115 crore. The minimum reserve system was adopted in 1956, replacing the proportional reserve
system that was followed earlier.
Important Tips
• In India, currency notes are issued by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), which is the monetary
authority in India.
• RBI Act 1934 empowers RBI to issue all the banknotes except 1 Rs. note. However, coins and 1
Rs. notes are issued by the Government of India under the coinage act 1909.

subject: Economy
question: Which one of the following statements correctly describes the meaning of legal tender money?

options.a: The money which is tendered in courts of law to defray the fee of legal cases
options.b: The money which a creditor is under compulsion to accept in settlement of his claims
options.c: The bank money in the form of cheques, drafts, bills of exchange etc.
options.d: The metallic money in circulation in a Country

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer
‘Legal tender’ is the money that is recognised by the law of the land, as valid for payment of debt.
It must be accepted for discharge of debt. The RBI Act of 1934, which gives the central bank the
sole right to issue bank notes, states that “Every bank note shall be legal tender at any place in
India in payment for the amount expressed therein”.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements: 1. Under the RBI Act, bank notes include both physical and digital forms. 2. RBI has the sole authority to issue bank notes in India. 3. Coins are issued under the RBI Act, 1934. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
RBI Amendment Act, 2022 included digital notes; coins are issued under the Coinage Act, not RBI
Act

subject: Economy
question: With reference to the evolution of the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), consider the following statements: 1. The Hilton Young Committee recommended the establishment of the RBI as a statutory body. 2. The RBI was initially established as a private shareholders' bank in 1935. 3. The RBI was nationalized on January 1, 1949, under the Reserve Bank (Transfer to Public Ownership) Act, 1948. How many of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct: The Hilton Young Committee (1924) recommended establishing the RBI as
a statutory body.
Statement 2 is correct: RBI started as a private shareholders' bank on April 1, 1935.
Statement 3 is correct: Nationalization occurred on Jan 1, 1949.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements regarding the capital and accounting of the RBI: 1. The initial capital of the RBI was Rs 5 crores, which continues to remain the same as of 2023. 2. Based on the Bimal Jalan Committee's recommendations, the RBI's accounting year was changed from July–June to April–March in 2021-22. 3. The definition of "Bank Note" was amended in 2022 to include digital notes. Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
All statements are correct.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to the RBI's role as a Debt Manager, consider the following statements: 1. The RBI manages and services Government of India Securities and State Government Securities. 2. Treasury Bills (T-Bills) are issued by both the Central and State Governments in India. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Statement 1 is correct: RBI manages securities for both Central and State governments.
Statement 2 is incorrect: There are no treasury bills issued by the State Governments; they are
only issued by the Government of India.

subject: Economy
question: In the context of India, the central bank’s function as the "Lender of Last Resort" refers to: 1. Lending to industry bodies when they fail to borrow from other sources. 2. Providing liquidity to banks facing a temporary crisis. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
"Lender of Last Resort" specifically refers to providing liquidity to banks in crisis, not directly to
trade/industry bodies

subject: Economy
question: The "Service Area Approach" was implemented under the purview of:
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Service Area Approach is linked to the Lead Bank Scheme

subject: Economy
question: With reference to Legal Tender in India, consider the following statements: 1. All bank notes are unlimited legal tender. 2. Coins have limited legal tender status. 3. Acceptance of legal tender is mandatory for settlement of transactions. Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is incorrect: While bank notes are legal tender, the obligation to accept them is not
absolute in all situations; hence they are not considered unlimited legal tender in every transaction
context.
Statement 2 is correct: Coins have limited legal tender status, meaning they are legal tender only
up to a prescribed amount.
Statement 3 is correct: Legal tender must be accepted for the settlement of monetary
transactions, unless specifically exempted by law.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements about the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC): 1. It was established based on the recommendations of the Urjit Patel Committee. 2. The Governor of the RBI serves as the ex-officio Chairperson. 3. Decisions are made by a simple majority, where the Chairperson has a casting vote in case of a tie. How many of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
All statements are correct.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to the Payments Regulatory Board (PRB), consider the following statements: 1. It is established under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007. 2. It is headed by the Revenue Secretary of India. 3. It includes members nominated by the Central Government. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct: PRB is under the PSS Act, 2007.
Statement 2 is incorrect: The PRB is headed by the Governor of the RBI.
Statement 3 is correct: It includes 3 members nominated by the government.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDC), consider the following statements: 1. It is possible to make payments in digital currency without using the SWIFT system. 2. A digital currency can be programmed with a specific time frame for spending. 3. The digital rupee appears as a liability on the RBI's balance sheet. Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct: CBDCs can facilitate cross-border payments through direct central bank–
to–central bank settlement mechanisms, thereby reducing dependence on the SWIFT system.=
Statement 2 is correct: CBDCs are programmable money, allowing restrictions based on time,
purpose, or conditions of use.
Statement 3 is correct: CBDCs are issued by the central bank and constitute a sovereign liability,
similar to physical currency.

subject: Economy
question: Regarding the "Unclaimed Deposits" framework, consider the following statements: 1. Deposits are classified as unclaimed if they are not claimed for 10 years. 2. These deposits are transferred to the Deposit Education and Awareness (DEA) Fund maintained by the Ministry of Finance. 3. The UDGAM portal allows depositors to track these unclaimed deposits. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Statement 2 is incorrect: The DEA Fund is maintained with the RBI, not the Ministry of Finance.

subject: Economy
question: The Banking Laws Amendment Act, 2025, introduced which of the following changes? 1. Increase in the maximum number of nominees for a bank account from one to four. 2. Increase in the maximum tenure of Board of Directors for Cooperative Banks from 8 to 10 years. 3. Redefinition of "Fortnight" for CRR calculation from "Saturday to Friday" to "1st–15th and 16th– last day of the month". Select the correct answer:
explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
All three are key provisions of the 2025 Amendment Act.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to Treasury Bills (T-Bills) in India, consider the following statements: 1. T-Bills are issued at a discount from the par value. 2. They are sold in India by the Reserve Bank of India on behalf of the government. 3. Retail investors can invest in T-Bills through a Demat account in the primary market. How many of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
T-Bills are zero-coupon (discounted), sold by RBI, and accessible to retail investors via platforms
like Retail Direct.

subject: Economy
question: The "Negotiated Dealing System-Order Matching (NDS-OM)" is:

options.a: A platform for trading corporate bonds.
options.b: A government securities trading platform of the RBI.
options.c: A portal for filing complaints against banks.

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
NDS-OM is an RBI platform for G-Secs trading.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements regarding the Board for Financial Supervision (BFS): 1. It functions as a committee of the Central Board of the RBI. 2. It carries out integrated supervision of banks and NBFCs. 3. It is chaired by the Finance Minister of India. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Statement 1 & 2 are correct: BFS is a committee for supervising banks/NBFCs.
Statement 3 is incorrect: It is chaired by the Governor of the RBI.

subject: Economy
question: What is the primary objective of the "Antardrishti" portal?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Antardrishti is a financial inclusion dashboard launched by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI). The
name translates to "insight" or "inner vision", reflecting its purpose of providing data-driven
insights into the progress and extent of financial inclusion across India.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements about Urban Cooperative Banks (UCBs): 1. The Banking Regulation (Amendment) Act, 2020, brought UCBs under the increased supervision of the RBI. 2. UCBs are exempt from maintaining CRR and SLR. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
The 2020 amendment did increase RBI oversight. UCBs are not exempt from CRR/SLR
requirements.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements: 1. Seigniorage arises from difference between face value and cost of printing. 2. RBI earns seigniorage from coin minting. 3. Interest earned on forex reserves is a source of RBI income. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Coin seigniorage goes to the Government.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) acting as a Banker to the Government, consider the following statements: 1. RBI has a legal obligation to act as the banker to both Central and all State Governments. 2. The Central Government is required to maintain a minimum cash balance with the RBI. 3. RBI directly accepts deposits and makes payments on behalf of the Government without involving any commercial banks. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is incorrect: RBI has an obligation and right to act as Banker to the Central
Government, but for State Governments, it acts based on an agreement. Currently, it acts as a
banker to all states except Sikkim.
Statement 2 is correct: Both Central and State Governments need to maintain minimum cash
balances with the RBI.
Statement 3 is incorrect: RBI does not directly accept deposits or make payments; it appoints
"agency banks" (Public and Private Sector Banks) to carry out these functions.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following states is the only one for which the RBI does NOT act as a banker?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Presently, the RBI acts as a banker to all states in India, except for the state of Sikkim.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to "Ways and Means Advances (WMA)", consider the following statements: 1. It is a loan facility provided by the RBI to the Centre and States to address temporary mismatches in cash balances. 2. The interest rate charged on WMA is fixed at 2% above the Repo Rate. 3. These advances must be repaid within a maximum period of 90 days. Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct: WMA is a loan facility to address temporary mismatches in cash balances.

Statement 2 is incorrect: The interest rate on WMA is the Repo Rate. If the borrowing exceeds
the WMA limit (Overdraft), the rate is 2% above the Repo Rate.
Statement 3 is correct: The maturity period for WMA is 90 days.

subject: Economy
question: The "Special Drawing Facility (SDF)" is a borrowing mechanism available to:

options.a: Commercial banks to borrow from RBI against SLR securities.
options.b: State Governments to borrow from RBI by pledging Central Government Securities.
options.c: Retail investors to borrow against their G-Sec holdings.
options.d: NBFCs to meet their liquidity requirements.

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
The Special Drawing Facility (SDF) enables State Governments to borrow short-term loans from
the RBI by pledging Central Government Securities as collateral. The interest rate on SDF is 1%
below the Repo Rate.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to Cash Management Bills (CMBs), which of the following statements is correct?

options.a: They are long-term instruments issued by both Centre and States.
options.b: They are issued for a maturity period of less than 91 days.
options.c: They carry a fixed coupon rate paid semi-annually.
options.d: They are primarily used to finance the fiscal deficit.

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
CMBs are short-term instruments issued by the Central Government (not states) for a maturity
period of less than 91 days. They are issued at a discount and redeemed at face value to address
temporary cash mismatches.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements regarding Government Securities (G-Secs) in India: 1. Treasury Bills (T-Bills) are issued by both the Central Government and State Governments. 2. T-Bills are issued at a discount to the face value and redeemed at par. 3. State Development Loans (SDLs) are long-term borrowing instruments issued by State Governments. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is incorrect: T-Bills are issued only by the Central Government.
Statement 2 is correct: T-Bills do not pay interest (coupon) but are issued at a discount and
redeemed at face value.

Statement 3 is correct: SDLs are long-term (more than 1 year) securities issued by State
Governments, similar to dated securities of the Centre.

subject: Economy
question: Which among the following is NOT an advantage of investing in Government Securities (G- Secs)?

options.b: They offer protection against inflation through Inflation-Indexed Bonds.
options.c: Interest income earned from G-Secs is completely tax-exempt.
options.d: They can be held in Demat form.

explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Interest income earned from G-Secs is generally taxable. They are risk-free (no default risk) and
can be held in Demat or physical form.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to the "Government Securities Act, 2006", consider the following statements: 1. The Act applies to securities issued by both the Central and State Governments. 2. National Savings Certificates (NSC) and Postal Savings Certificates are included under this Act. 3. The RBI is the primary issuer of G-Secs on behalf of the government under this framework. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct: The Act applies to G-Secs issued by both Centre and States.
Statement 2 is incorrect: National Savings Certificates and Postal Savings Certificates are
explicitly excluded from the Act.
Statement 3 is correct: RBI is the issuer for T-Bills, Dated Bonds, and SDLs.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following risks is generally NOT associated with investing in G-Secs?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
G-Secs carry practically zero default risk because they are backed by a sovereign guarantee.
However, they are subject to market risk (price fluctuations), reinvestment risk, and liquidity risk.

subject: Economy
question: In the context of "STRIPS" in the G-Sec market, what does the term "Stripping" refer to?

options.a: Selling G-Secs in the secondary market at a massive discount.
options.b: Separating the cash flows of a regular G-Sec into independent individual securities of interest and principal.
options.c: The process of converting physical G-Secs into Demat form.
options.d: Reducing the maturity period of a bond to manage interest rate risk.

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
STRIPS (Separate Trading of Registered Interest and Principal of Securities) involves separating
the periodic interest (coupon) payments and the final principal repayment of a bond into separate
zero-coupon securities.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to Inflation-Indexed Bonds (IIBs), consider the following statements: 1. They provide protection to investors from uncertainty regarding inflation for both principal and interest. 2. The interest on IIBs is calculated on the inflation-adjusted principal. 3. Capital gains on IIBs are exempt from taxation in India. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Statement 1 and 2 are correct: IIBs protect both principal and interest against inflation, with
interest calculated on the adjusted principal.
Statement 3 is incorrect: Income and capital gains from IIBs are taxable, just like normal bonds.

subject: Economy
question: How do Capital Indexed Bonds differ from Inflation-Indexed Bonds (IIBs)?

options.a: Only principal is protected against inflation in Capital Indexed Bonds.
options.b: Only interest is protected against inflation in Capital Indexed Bonds.
options.c: Interest is paid on a fixed principal in IIBs.
options.d: Capital Indexed Bonds were first issued in 2013.

explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
In Capital Indexed Bonds, only the principal amount is protected against inflation, whereas in IIBs,
both principal and interest payments are protected.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to "Sovereign Green Bonds", consider the following statements: 1. The proceeds are deposited in the Consolidated Fund of India (CFI). 2. They are eligible to be considered as SLR (Statutory Liquidity Ratio) securities. 3. Non-Resident investors are not allowed to invest in these bonds. Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.

Statement 1 is correct: Proceeds go to the CFI.
Statement 2 is correct: They are eligible for SLR.
Statement 3 is incorrect: Non-Residents (under the Fully Accessible Route) are allowed to invest
in Sovereign Green Bonds without any limit.

subject: Economy
question: The term "Greenium" in the context of Green Bonds refers to:

options.a: The extra tax incentive provided by the government to green project investors.
options.b: The savings (lower yield) realized by the issuer due to higher demand for green bonds.
options.c: The premium paid by the government to banks for financing green energy.
options.d: The penalty imposed on companies for not meeting green investment targets.

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Greenium (Green + Premium) refers to the lower yield (or higher price) that investors are willing
to accept for green bonds compared to normal bonds due to high demand, resulting in lower
borrowing costs for the issuer.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following projects is excluded from financing through Sovereign Green Bonds?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Excluded projects include nuclear power, direct waste incineration, landfill projects, large
hydropower (>25 MW), and fossil fuel-based projects

subject: Economy
question: With reference to the "Retail Direct Scheme" of the RBI, consider the following statements: 1. It allows individual retail investors to buy G-Secs directly from the RBI in the primary market. 2. Only Resident Indians are eligible to open a Retail Direct Gilt Account. 3. Investors can trade G-Secs in the secondary market through this platform. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Statement 1 and 3 are correct: Retail investors can buy directly in the primary market and trade
in the secondary market.
Statement 2 is incorrect: Both Resident and Non-Resident individuals can open these accounts.

subject: Economy
question: The "E-Kuber" platform of the RBI is primarily used for:

options.a: Providing a grievance redressal mechanism for bank customers.
options.b: Core Banking Solution (CBS) operations including G-Sec auctions and maintaining CRR balances.
options.c: Trading of corporate stocks and derivatives.

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
E-Kuber is the Core Banking Solution of RBI used for G-Sec auctions, maintaining CRR balances,
inter-bank settlements, and providing WMA to the government.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements regarding the "Negotiated Dealing System-Order Matching (NDS-OM)": 1. It is a government securities trading platform owned by the RBI. 2. It is primarily used for trading G-Secs in the secondary market. 3. It is operated by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI). Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
NDS-OM is owned by the RBI and used for secondary market trading of G-Secs. However, it is
operated by the Clearing Corporation of India Limited (CCIL), not SEBI.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following is correct regarding the E-Kuber platform?

options.a: All retail investors can now participate in T-Bill auctions directly on E-Kuber.
options.b: All government payments above Rs 75 crores must be made through E-Kuber.
options.c: Foreign Central Banks can now trade Indian SDLs on E-Kuber.
options.d: E-Kuber has been integrated with the SWIFT system for international trade.

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
As of July 2025, all payments made by Government ministries and departments above Rs 75 crores
must be made through the E-Kuber platform.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to the RBI's regulatory powers over Public Sector Banks (PSBs) versus Private Banks, consider the following statements: 1. RBI has the power to supersede the Board of Directors of both PSBs and Private Banks. 2. Provisions of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 apply differently to PSBs compared to Private Banks. 3. RBI can force the merger of Private Banks but cannot do so for PSBs. Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is incorrect: RBI can supersede the Board of Private Banks but has limited powers to
do so for PSBs.
Statement 2 and 3 are correct: RBI's powers over PSBs are limited as they are also regulated by
the government and governed by Nationalization Acts. RBI cannot force mergers of PSBs but can
do so for Private Banks.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following entities is NOT regulated by the RBI?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer
Credit Rating Agencies (like ICRA, CRISIL) are regulated by SEBI, not RBI. AIFIs, ARCs, and CICs
are regulated by the RBI.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements regarding Credit Information Companies (CICs) in India: 1. CICs are regulated by the RBI under the CICRA Act, 2005. 2. All banks and NBFCs must become members of all existing CICs. 3. TransUnion CIBIL is a government-owned Credit Information Company. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Statement 1 and 2 are correct: CICs are regulated under CICRA 2005, and credit providers must
be members of all CICs.
Statement 3 is incorrect: TransUnion CIBIL is owned by an American MNC, TransUnion; it is not
government-owned.

subject: Economy
question: Under the "National Financial Information Registry (NFIR)" proposal, how does it differ from existing CICs?

options.a: NFIR will only collect data on agricultural loans, while CICs collect all data.
options.b: NFIR will be owned by the RBI and collect comprehensive financial info (loans, tax, bonds), unlike CICs which focus on loans.
options.c: CICs provide data for free, while NFIR will charge a high fee.
options.d: NFIR will only cater to international investors.

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
The proposed NFIR would be owned by the RBI and collect a wider range of financial information
(loans, tax payments, bonds) compared to CICs which primarily collect loan information.

subject: Economy
question: What is the "Grameen Credit Score" mentioned in the Budget 2025-26?

options.a: A score for large corporate farmers based on export data.
options.b: A credit scoring mechanism for SHG members and rural people based on land ownership, UPI activity, and utility bills.
options.c: A ranking system for Rural Cooperative Banks.
options.d: A government subsidy for farmers with low credit scores.

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
The Grameen Credit Score is to be developed by PSBs to assess the creditworthiness of SHG
members and rural residents using unconventional parameters like saving patterns in SHGs and
utility bill payments.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to the Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation (DICGC), consider the following statements: 1. It covers deposits in all commercial, regional, rural, and cooperative banks. 2. NBFCs and Rural Cooperative Societies are also covered under this insurance. 3. The insurance limit is Rs 5 lakh per depositor per bank. Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Statement 1 and 3 are correct: Coverage includes commercial and cooperative banks, with a limit
of Rs 5 lakh.
Statement 2 is incorrect: NBFCs and Rural Cooperative Societies are NOT covered.

subject: Economy
question: The "UDGAM" portal launched by the RBI is intended for:

options.a: Facilitating applications for new bank licenses.
options.b: A centralized search for unclaimed deposits across multiple banks.
options.c: Real-time monitoring of the call money market.
options.d: Registering complaints against digital payment providers.

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
UDGAM (Unclaimed Deposits-Gateway to Access information) facilitates users in searching for
unclaimed deposits/accounts across multiple banks in one place.

subject: Economy
question: The "PRAVAAAH" portal is designed to:

options.a: Track the flow of FDI into the manufacturing sector.
options.b: Simplify and streamline application processes for various regulatory approvals.
options.c: Monitor the liquidity coverage ratio of systemically important banks.
options.d: Facilitate the auction of Sovereign Green Bonds.

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
PRAVAAAH is a web-based centralized portal to simplify and streamline application processes.

subject: Economy
question: The "Housing Price Index (HPI)" published by the RBI has which of the following as its base year?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
The Housing Price Index (HPI) measures price changes in 10 cities and has a base year of 2010-11.

subject: Economy
question: Which index measures changes in the prices charged by banks for their services?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
The Banking Services Price Index measures changes in the prices charged by banks for their
services, with 2011-12 as the base year.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following variables: 1. Economic situation 2. Employment 3. Price level 4. Income 5. Spending The "RBI Consumer Confidence Survey" measures expectations on how many of the above variables?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
The survey measures consumer confidence and expectations on all five economic variables:
economic situation, employment, price level, income, and spending.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following measures can be taken by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) to increase liquidity in the economy?

options.d: Decreasing borrowing limit under Marginal Standing Facility

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Targeted Long Term Repo Operations (TLTROs) facility allows banks to borrow money from the
RBI at the repo rate for one and three years. This improves capacity of banks to extend cheaper
credit to businesses.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements with regard to the monetary policy tools and their impact: 1. Monetary policy tool includes direct lending to banks. 2. Open market operations (OMO) refer to the government directly buying or selling short-term treasuries and other securities in the open market. 3. Reducing CRR and SLR requirements for banks may lead to a decline in the interest rates. How many of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct. Monetary Policy tools include open market operations, direct lending to
banks, bank reserve requirements, unconventional emergency lending programs, and managing
market expectations—subject to the central bank’s credibility.
Statement 2 is incorrect. Open market operations (OMO) refer to a central bank and not the
government buying or selling short-term treasuries and other securities in the open market.
Statement 3 is correct. Lowering reserve requirements (CRR and SLR) releases more capital for
the banks to offer loans or to buy other assets and hence the interest rate declines.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to Market Stabilization scheme, consider the following statements: 1. It is a monetary policy intervention by the RBI. 2. It infuses excess liquidity in market by buying government securities. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.

Statement 1 is correct. Market Stabilization scheme (MSS) is a monetary policy intervention by
the RBI. These securities are owned by the government though they are issued by the RBI.
Statement 2 is incorrect. It withdraws excess liquidity (or money supply) by selling government
securities in the economy. The MSS was introduced in April 2004.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following instruments of Monetary Policy Transmission are available with the Reserve Bank of India? 1. Open Market Operations 2. Varying of Cash Reserve Ratio and Statutory Liquidity Ratio 3. Sterilization by buying and selling of foreign currency in the exchange market. 4. Moral suasion Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Point 1 is correct. Open Market Operations (OMO) RBI purchases (or sells) government securities
to the general public in a bid to increase (or decrease) the stock of high-powered money in the
economy.
Point 2 is correct. A high (or low) value of CRR or SLR increases (or decreases) the value of reserve
deposit ratio, thus diminishing (or increasing) the value of money multiplier and money supply in
the economy in a similar fashion.
Point 4 is correct. Moral Suasion can be undertaken by RBI to persuade commercial banks to
follow the monetary policy directives given by the Monetary Policy Committee.
Point 3 is incorrect. Sterilization is one of the important functions of RBI where it sterilizes the
economy against adverse external shocks. However, it is not an instrument of monetary policy
transmission.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to Monetary Policy Committee (MPC), consider the following statements: 1. The committee is constituted by the Reserve Bank of India. 2. The MPC determines the policy interest rate required to achieve the inflation target. 3. The decisions of the committee are based on consensus. How many of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is incorrect: The MPC is a six-member committee constituted by the Central
government by notification in the official gazette.

Statement 2 is correct. The MPC determines the policy interest rate required to achieve the
inflation target.
Statement 3 is incorrect: Each member of the MPC has one vote and in case of equality of votes,
the Governor has a second or casting vote. Thus, the decisions are not based on the consensus.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements regarding the Marginal Standing Facility (MSF) of RBI: 1. It is used by all financial institutions. 2. It is a part of the liquidity adjustment facility. 3. It is a cheaper route to fulfil the overnight requirement of funds. 4. Banks use this route only if they exhaust all other channels to raise short-term funds. How many of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is incorrect. Marginal Standing Facility is only for banks (not for other financial
institutions).
Statement 2 is incorrect. Though it is on the lines of the Liquidity Adjustment Facility (LAF) but it
is not a part of it. RBI’s LAF which helps banks to adjust their daily liquidity mismatches.
Statement 3 is incorrect. MSF is a penal rate and thus not a cheap way and remains higher than
the repo rate. It is generally at a rate of 1 percent higher than the current repo rate, but it is not
fixed and can be changed.
Statement 4 is correct. Banks use this facility only if they exhaust all other means to raise short
term funds.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to Liquidity Adjustment Facility (LAF), which of the following statements is/are Correct? 1. LAF was introduced on the recommendations of the Narsimham Committee on Banking Sector Reforms. 2. Through LAF, RBI manages the inflation in the economy. 3. LAF in recent years, has been extended to certain Scheduled Urban Cooperative Banks (UCBs). Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct. The LAF was introduced in RBI in 1998 based on the recommendations of
Narasimham Committee on Banking Sector Reforms.
Statement 2 is correct. Through LAF tools like Repo, the RBI manages the inflation in the range
between 2 and 6%.

Statement 3 is correct. From 2014, Liquidity Adjustment Facility (LAF) was extended to Scheduled
Urban Cooperative Banks (UCBs) which are Core Banking System (CBS) enabled.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following are the components of Statutory Liquidity Ratio (SLR)? 1. Cash 2. Gold 3. Treasury Bills 4. Dated securities issued under market stabilisation schemes 5. State development loans Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Statutory Liquidity Ratio or SLR is a minimum percentage of deposits that a commercial bank has
to maintain in the form of liquid cash, gold or other securities. It is basically the reserve
requirement that banks are expected to keep before offering credit to customers. Following are
the components of SLR: Cash Gold and unencumbered securities. Treasury bills of the
Government of India. Dated securities issued under market borrowing programme and market
stabilisation schemes (MSS). State Development Loans (SDLs) of the State Governments issued
from time to time under the market borrowing program; and any other instrument as may be
notified by the Reserve Bank of India.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following entities can take loans through both Marginal Standing Facility (MSF) and Liquidity Adjustment Facility (LAF) of the RBI? 1. Schedules commercial banks. 2. Regional rural banks. 3. Non-Banking Financial Company. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Option 1 is correct. All Scheduled Commercial Banks (including Regional Rural Banks) and
Primary Dealers (PDs) are eligible to participate in the Repo and Reverse Repo auctions i.e.
Liquidity Adjustment Facility of RBI. The banks under Marginal standing facility can borrow funds
by pledging government securities within the limits of the statutory liquidity ratio. Scheduled
commercial banks and Regional rural banks (RRBs) can borrow under this window.
Option 2 is correct. RBI recently allowed regional rural banks (RRBs) to access funds under
liquidity adjustment facility (LAF), marginal standing facility (MSF) and call or notice money

market, aimed at facilitating better liquidity management for these lenders. Earlier RRBs were not
permitted to access the liquidity windows of the RBI as well as the call or notice market.
Option 3 is incorrect. Unlike banks, NBFCs cannot borrow directly from the RBI’s Liquidity
Adjustment Facility and Marginal Standing Facility.

subject: Economy
question: To control inflation in an economy, which of the following steps can be taken by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI)?

options.a: Purchase government securities under Open Market Operations

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Option b is correct. Cash/Currency Reserve Ratio (CRR) is the amount of funds that banks have
to keep with RBI. The RBI uses CRR to drain out excessive money from system. If CRR is increased
more cash of banks have to be kept as reserve with RBI, hence limiting cash in circulation thereby
controlling Inflation.
Option a is incorrect. Open market operations, or OMOs, are the purchase and sale of G-Secs by
the RBI on the Centre’s behalf to streamline money supply and interest rates. In case of excess
liquidity in the market, RBI issues these securities via auctions. Open market purchases increase
money supply and results in excess cash in circulation, thus promoting Inflation. To control
inflation, the RBI sells the securities in the money market which sucks out excess liquidity from
the market. As the amount of liquid cash decreases, demand goes down.
Option c is incorrect. Statutory Liquidity Ratio (SLR) is the amount that banks have to maintain
as a stipulated proportion of their net demand and time liabilities (NDTL) in form of liquid
assets like cash, gold and unencumbered securities, treasury bills, dated securities etc. Decrease
in SLR leads to increasing cash in circulation thereby promoting Inflation.
Option d is incorrect. The term ‹Repo› refers to repurchase option or agreement. It is a monetary
tool used by the RBI to allow commercial banks to borrow money, when in need, against collaterals
such as government bonds and treasury bills. If Repo rate in decreased, Banks will find borrow
money from RBI becomes easier. Hence more cash in hand, more in circulation thus promoting
Inflation.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements with reference to RBI’s ‘Margin Requirements’ on loans: 1. It is one of the Quantitative methods of credit control. 2. It refers to the difference between average loan interest rate and the average deposit interest rate of banks. 3. In case of inflation, the margin requirement is increased so that demand for loans is decreased. 4. The margin requirements against specific securities are determined solely by individual bank. How many of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is incorrect. Margin requirement is one of the Qualitative or selective methods of
credit control used by RBI to stabilize the economy from inflation or deflation.
Statement 2 is incorrect. Margin requirement refers to the difference between the current
value of the security offered for loan (called collateral) and the value of loan granted. Margin
against a particular security is reduced or increased in order to encourage or to discourage the
flow of credit to a particular sector. It varies from 20% to 80%. Higher the margin lesser will be
the loan sanctioned.
Statement 4 is incorrect: The margin requirements against specific securities are determined by
the Central Bank. Changes in margin requirements are designed to influence the flow of credit
against specific commodities. A rise in the margin requirement results in a contraction in the
borrowing value of the security and similarly, a fall in the margin requirement results in expansion
in the borrowing value of the security.
Statement 3 is correct. In case of inflation, the margin requirement is increased so that demand
for loans are decreased. In case of deflation, margin requirements are decreased so that demand
for loans are increased.
Important Tips
Margin means that proportion of the value of security against which loan is not given. A loan
is sanctioned against Collateral Security. For example- a person mortgages his house worth one
crore rupees with the bank for a loan of 80 lakh rupees. The margin requirement in this case
will be 20 lakh rupees. The commercial banks generally advance loans to their customers
against some security or securities offered by the borrower and acceptable to banks. More
generally, the commercial banks do not lend up to the full amount of the security but lend an
amount less than its value.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following is/are the objective and impacts of ‘Long Term Repo Operation’ of Reserve Bank of India? 1. To let banks, borrow one to three-year funds from the central bank at the repo rate. 2. To increase marginal cost of funds-based lending rate of the banks without reducing policy rates. 3. To undertake maturity transformation and augment credit flows to productive sectors. 4. To reduce the spread between long-term Government securities bonds and corporate bonds. Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct. Under on-tap targeted long-term repo operations (TLTRO), RBI will
conduct term repos of one-year and three-year tenors of appropriate sizes for up to a total
amount of Rs 1 lakh crore at the policy repo rate. Funds through LTRO will be provided at the repo
rate. This means that banks can avail one year and three-year loans at the same interest rate of
one day repo.

Statement 2 is incorrect. Long Term Repo Operation will not increase marginal cost of funds-
based lending rate of the banks without reducing policy rates. On the contrary, it will ensure that
banks reduce their marginal cost of fundsbased lending rate, without reducing policy rates. As
banks get long-term funds at lower rates, their cost of funds falls. In turn, they reduce interest
rates for borrowers.
Statement 3 is correct. Long Term Repo Operation will assure banks about the availability of
durable liquidity at reasonable cost relative to prevailing market conditions. Further encourage
banks to undertake maturity transformation smoothly and seamlessly so as to augment credit
flows to productive sectors.
Statement 4 is incorrect. Long Term Repo Operation does not intend to reduce the spread
between long-term Government securities bonds and corporate bonds.
Important Tips
Operation twist involves buying long term securities and selling short term securities. It will
reduce the yield of long-term securities compared to that of the short-term ones. This yield
impact is the objective of Operation Twist.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements: 1. If Reserve bank of India reduces the cash reserve ratio it will decrease the money supply in economy. 2. The lowering of bank rate by the Reserve bank of India leads to more liquidity in the market. 3. An increase in Statutory Liquidity Ratio rate means that commercial banks shall have to invest more money in Government and other approved securities. 4. Loan-to-value is a ratio used in mortgage lending to determine the amount necessary to put in a down-payment. How many of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is incorrect: The Reserve Bank of India or RBI mandates that banks store a proportion
of their deposits in the form of cash so that the same can be given to the bank’s customers if the
need arises. The percentage of cash required to be kept in reserves, vis-a-vis a bank’s total
deposits, is called the Cash Reserve Ratio. The cash reserve is either stored in the bank’s vault or
is sent to the RBI. Banks do not get any interest on the money that is with the RBI under the CRR
requirements.
Statement 2 is correct: Any reduction in the bank rate and the repo rate will lead to borrowers
getting loans at lower interest rates. The lowering of the Bank Rate makes the domestic banks
borrow money at a cheap rate which in turn increases the liquidity in the market.
Statement 3 is correct: The SLR regulation is binding on all the commercial banks in India. RBI is
with the power to increase the SLR rate, as and when it desires to do so. An increase in SLR rate
means that commercial banks shall have to invest more money in Government and other
approved securities which deplete lendable sources of the banks.

Statement 4 is correct: Loan-to-value (LTV) is an often-used ratio in mortgage lending to
determine the amount necessary to put in a down-payment and whether a lender will extend
credit to a borrower.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to the monetary policy tools of the Reserve Bank of India, consider the following statements: 1. A Reverse Repo Rate is the interest rate at which the Central Bank gives loans to commercial banks. 2. Reserve Bank of India conducts Variable Rate Reverse Repo (VRRR) auctions to increase liquidity in the banking system. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is incorrect: Reverse repo rate is the interest rate paid to commercial banks when
they deposit their excess funds in the central bank or when the central bank borrows money
from them. Repo rate is the rate at which the central bank gives loans to commercial banks against
government securities.
Statement 2 is incorrect: The decision of Reserve Bank India (RBI) to conduct variable rate
reverse repo (VRRR) auctions aims to absorb excess liquidity from the banking system.

subject: Economy
question: With reference to Open Market Operations (OMOs), consider the following statements: 1. It can be used for both increasing or decreasing the supply of money in the banking system. 2. It is conducted through E-Kuber platform of the Reserve bank of India. 3. Operation Twist is a type of OMO under which sale and purchase of Governments securities is conducted simultaneously. How many of the above given statements are incorrect?
explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Open market operations (OMOs) are an effective quantitative policy tool at the disposal of RBI. It
helps in maintaining the impact on inflation and interest rate levels.
Statement 1 is correct: OMOs refers to the purchase and sale of government securities(G-sec),
done by RBI on the government’s behalf. When G-sec is bought, it increases the liquidity or money
supply in the market on the other the hand when G-sec is sold, money supply is decreased, or
liquidity is drained from the market.

Statement 2 is correct: RBI carries out the OMO through commercial banks and does not directly
deal with the public. Eligible participants are required to key in their bids on RBI’s core banking
electronic solution platform E-Kuber.
Statement 3 is correct: Simultaneous purchase and sale of government securities under OMOs
is popularly known as Operation Twist. It involves buying long-end debt while selling short-tenor
bonds to keep borrowing costs down.
Important Tips
Operation Twist is a way employed by the central bank to manage yields in the market. It is a
program of quantitative easing used by the RBI that was first introduced by the Federal Reserves
in US in 1961. Since prices and yields move in opposite directions, by purchasing longer-term
bonds, the RBI can help drive the bond prices up and yields down. At the same time, selling
shorter-term bonds should cause their yields to go up (since their prices would fall). In
combination, these two actions twist the shape of the yield curve.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following pairs: Monetary Policy stance of the Reserve Bank of India Action taken by the Reserve Bank 1. Accommodative Stance RBI may lower the interest rates 2. Neutral Stance The policy priority of RBI is equal on both inflation and economic growth. 3. Hawkish Stance RBI increases the lending rate of interest to the banks. 4. Calibrated Tightening RBI always reduces the Repo Rate How many pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer
Central banks’ decisions rely on an assessment of their monetary policy stance.
Pair 1 is correct: An accommodative stance means the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is
willing to either lower rates or keep them unchanged Accommodative monetary policy is a
strategy used by central banks that is aimed at keeping interest rates low in order to infuse more
cash into the economy to boost growth.
Pair 2 is correct: Neutral stance indicates that central bank may cut or hike interest rate. Range
of rates consistent with full employment, trend growth, and stable prices. Policy priority is equal
on both inflation and economic growth.
Pair 3 is correct: Hawkish stance indicates the tight monetary policy where main aim of central
bank is to control inflation. RBI is in favour of high interest rate to curb the money supply in
market.
Pair 4 is incorrect: Calibrated tightening refers to the no obligation of increasing repo rate at
every policy meeting in current rate cycle. So, RBI will either keep repo rate unchanged or may
increase it.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements: 1. An increase in the Cash Reserve Ratio may reduce the credit availability in the economy. 2. An increase in Repo Rates may result in an increase in the cost of borrowing. 3. Lower Bank Rates can help to lower the cost of funds for borrowers. 4. An increase in the Statutory Liquidity Ratio will tend to reduce money supply in economy. How many of the above given statements are correct?
explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct: If the RBI decides to increase the CRR, then bank have to keep higher cash.
This will reduce the credit in the economy as the banks now have less money to lend, less lending
means less borrowing and investment and hence reduction in income and aggregate demand.
Statement 2 is correct: An increase in repo rates means an increase in the cost of borrowing. This
is because when the repo rate rises, the borrowing cost for banking institutions also rises, which
is passed on to account holders in the form of higher loan and deposit interest rates.
Statement 3 is correct: Managing the bank rate is a method by which central banks affect
economic activity. Lower bank rates can help to expand the economy by lowering the cost of funds
for borrowers, and higher bank rates help to reign in the economy when inflation is higher than
desired.
Statement 4 is correct: As SLR is increased banks will have to keep more liquidity with themselves.
So, the less amount of funds will be available to the banks for credit creation. The total money
supply in the economy will decrease, this will lead to less demand.
Important Tips
• Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR): The average daily balance that a bank is required to maintain with
the Reserve Bank as a per cent of its net demand and time liabilities (NDTL).
• Repos or Repurchase Agreements is an instrument which allows banks to borrow money from
the RBI to manage short term needs of liquidity against the selling of government securities
with an agreement to repurchase the same government securities at a predetermined date and
rate. The rate at which the RBI lends to the banks is called Repo Rate.
• Bank Rate: The rate at which the Reserve Bank is ready to buy or rediscount bills of exchange
or other commercial papers.
• Statutory Liquidity Ratio or SLR is a minimum percentage of deposits that a commercial bank
has to maintain in the form of liquid cash, gold or other securities.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following is the main aim of Standing Deposit Facility (SDF), recently introduced by the Reserve Bank of India?

options.a: To increase demand in the economy by increasing the money supply.
options.b: To absorb excess liquidity from the banking system in India.
options.c: To ensure effective monetary policy transmission at ground level.
options.d: To ensure that banks have enough capital to handle certain number of losses.

explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
When banks have excess funds, they lend it to the RBI at the reverse repo rate in order to get
returns from these extra funds. To execute the reverse repo rate functions, RBI needs
government securities as collaterals. SDF is a monetary tool that allows banks to park their access
liquidity with RBI without any collateral. RBI has introduced this tool to absorb excess liquidity in
the market as it plays an important role in determining the policy rates. Due to demonetization,
funds with banks increased majorly and recently RBI also increased the reverse repo rate, these
changes started attracting more funds towards the reverse repo operations. As the collaterals with
RBI are limited in nature, it introduced a new facility known as Standing deposit facility, that allows
RBI to execute reverse repo operations without using collaterals to absorb excess funds in the
banking sector.

subject: Economy
question: Which one of the following will be the most likely implication of the increase in repo rate by 1% by Reserve Bank of India?

options.a: Increase in credit offtake in the market by corporates and investors.
options.b: Yield of the government bonds will definitely decrease in the long run.
options.c: Borrowing costs for individuals and businesses may increase.
options.d: Inflation will increase due to excessive money circulation in the market.

explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
In India, if the Repo rate is increased by 1%, the main implication is that it would become more
expensive for commercial banks to borrow money from the Reserve Bank of India (RBI). This
makes it more expensive for commercial banks to lend money to their customers, such as
individuals and businesses. As such, borrowing costs for individuals and businesses would
increase, which could lead to a slowdown in economic growth.
Option a is incorrect: An increase in the repo rate actually leads to a decrease in credit offtake
in the market. Commercial banks have to pay a higher interest rate to borrow money from the
RBI, and this cost is passed on to their customers in the form of higher lending rates.
Option b is incorrect: An increase in the repo rate is likely to increase the yield of government
bonds in the long run, the increase in the repo rate is likely to reduce inflationary pressures,
which may increase investor confidence in the economy and lead to an increase in the demand
for government bonds, thereby increasing their yield.
Option d is incorrect: An increase in the repo rate is often used by the RBI to control inflation
by reducing the amount of money available for lending and spending. This reduces the demand
for goods and services, which may lead to a decrease in the prices of goods and services, thus
breaking the inflationary cycle.
Important Tips
The decision to increase the Repo rate in India is taken by the Monetary Policy Committee
(MPC), which takes into account various factors, including inflation, economic growth, and
global economic conditions, before making its decision

subject: Economy
question: With reference to the liquidity management, consider the following instruments: 1. Liquidity Adjustment Facility 2. Marginal Standing Facility 3. Call Money Market 4. Notice Money market How many of the above given instruments are accessible to Regional Rural banks?
explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct: Liquidity Adjustment Facility (LAF) has been extended to RRB. It enables
banks to borrow money through repurchase agreements (repo) or banks to lend to the RBI using
reverse repo contracts.
Statement 2 is correct: Marginal Standing Facility (MSF) has been extended to RRBs. In this,
planned commercial banks can lend extra amount of immediate cash from the RBI by dipping into
their Statutory Liquidity Ratio (SLR) collection up till a limit at a penal rate of interest.
Statement 3 is correct: Call Money Market has been extended to RRB. Call money is also referred
to as the money at call. It is a short-term loan which is due to be paid immediately in full as and
when demanded by the lender generally transacted on overnight basis.
Statement 4 is correct: Notice money market has been extended to RRB. It is a short-term loan
which is due to be paid after serving the notice. under notice money market, funds are transacted
for the period between 2 days and 14 days.
Important Tips
• The maintaining of liquid assets by the commercial banks at the rate fixed by RBI is called
Statutory Liquidity Ratio.
• SLR is a statutory provision under Section 24 of the Banking Regulation Act 1949 and it is in
addition to the Cash Reserve Ratio to be maintained by the bank.
• Every scheduled bank in India requires maintaining liquid assets in the form of cash, gold and
unencumbered approved securities and value of which shall not be less than 25 percent of
demand and time liabilities of the bank.
• The implication of change in SLR is same as the change in CRR in regulating the expansion of
credit. The SLR regulation is binding on all the commercial banks in India

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements with reference to inflation targeting in India: 1. The announcement of an official target range for inflation is known as inflation targeting. 2. Inflation Target must be set by the Reserve Bank of India every year. 3. The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth. 4. When the RBI fails to meet the inflation target for three consecutive Quarters, its Monetary Policy Committee must resign. How many of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Statement 1 is correct. The announcement of an official target range for inflation is known as
inflation targeting. It is done by the Central Bank in an economy as a part of their monetary policy
to realise the objective of a stable rate of inflation (the Government of India asked the RBI to
perform this function in the early 1970s
Statement 2 is incorrect. The Reserve Bank of India Act provides for the inflation target to be
set by the Government of India, in consultation with the central bank, once in every five years.
Statement 3 is correct. Monetary policy refers to the use of monetary instruments under the
control of the central bank to regulate magnitudes such as interest rates, money supply and
availability of credit with a view to achieving the ultimate objective of the economic policy. The
primary objective of the monetary policy is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind
the objective of growth. Price stability is a necessary pre-condition to sustainable growth.
Statement 4 is incorrect. When the RBI fails to meet the inflation target, it will send a report
to the central government stating reasons and the remedial actions that will be taken.
Important Tips
A breach of the tolerance level for three consecutive quarters will constitute a failure of
monetary policy. “The central government notified the following as factors that constitute
failure to achieve the inflation target: the average inflation is more than the upper tolerance
level of the inflation target for any three consecutive quarters the average inflation is less than
the lower tolerance level for any three consecutive quarters”.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following measures are likely to be adopted by the Government/RBI to tackle inflation? 1. Increase in indirect taxes 2. Surplus budget 3. Increasing Cash Reserve Ratio 4. Decreasing the Repo Rate Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Option 1 is incorrect. Increase in Indirect tax rate like Goods and service tax (GST), excise duty
will push the price further up. So, reducing the indirect tax rate is a measure used by the
government to curb inflation.
Option 2 is correct. Surplus budget means less expenditure than receipts. It reduces the money
supply and government demand for goods and services. The price level will be brought down due
to this.
Option 3 is correct. Scheduled banks are required to keep a certain percentage of their Net Time
and Demand Deposits with RBI. Thus, the increased CRR means less money with banks to lend
which will reduce the money supply in the economy.

Option 4 is incorrect. Repo rate is the rate at which the commercial banks borrow money from
RBI by mortgaging their dated government securities and treasury bills. The decreased repo rate
means the cheaper credit access to banks from RBI which will ultimately increase the money
supply in the economy.

subject: Economy
question: Which one of the following activities of the Reserve Bank of India is considered to be part of ‘sterilization’?

options.b: Oversight of settlement and payment systems
options.c: Debt and cash management for the Central and State Governments
options.d: Regulating the functions of Non-banking Financial Institutions

explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Sterilization refers to the actions taken by a central bank to offset the impact of its foreign
exchange operations on the domestic money supply. When a central bank intervenes in the
foreign exchange market by buying or selling foreign currencies, it affects the domestic money
supply. Open Market Operations (OMO) is one of the primary tools used by central banks,
including the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), to conduct monetary policy. In OMO, the central bank
buys or sells government securities (bonds) in the open market to influence the liquidity in the
economy. When the RBI conducts OMO, it impacts the money supply in the economy. If the RBI
buys government securities, it injects money into the system, increasing the money supply. To
prevent this injection of money from creating inflationary pressures, the RBI engages in
sterilization. Sterilization involves the simultaneous sale or purchase of other securities, typically
treasury bills, to offset the impact of the initial open market operation.
Important Tips
• Oversight of settlement payment systems refers to the role of the Reserve Bank of India in
supervising and ensuring the smooth functioning of payment and settlement systems in the
country. Payment systems facilitate the transfer of funds between individuals, businesses, and
financial institutions.
• Debt and cash management for the Central and State Governments involves the Reserve Bank
of India’s role in managing the issuance, redemption, and servicing of government debt, as well
as overseeing the cash flow operations of the government. This function is important for
maintaining government finances and liquidity.
• The Reserve Bank of India regulates and supervises non-banking financial institutions (NBFIs)
such as non-banking financial companies (NBFCs). NBFIs play a crucial role in the financial
system by providing various financial services.

subject: Economy
question: If the RBI decides to adopt an expansionist monetary policy, which of the following would it not do? 1. Cut and optimize the Statutory Liquidity Ratio 2. Increase the Marginal Standing Facility Rate 3. Cut the Bank Rate and Repo Rate Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Expansionary monetary policy is when the Reserve Bank of India uses its monetary policy tools to
stimulate the economy. That increases the money supply, lowers interest rates, and increases
demand.
Statements 1 is incorrect. A reduction in SLR would mean higher liquidity for the banks, so they
would have more funds to lend. This would tend to reduce lending rates and increase demand
in the economy. The SLR is the reserve that the commercial banks in India are required to
maintain in the form of cash, gold reserves, government approved securities before providing
credit to the customers.
Statement 2 is correct. Marginal Standing facility (MSF) is a special window for the commercial
banks to borrow from the RBI against approved government securities, in case of an emergency
such as an acute cash shortage. MSF rate is generally higher than Repo rate. An increase in the
MSF rate leads to higher borrowing cost for the banks and thus, reduces money supply in the
economy. It is a contractionary monetary policy.
Statements 3 is incorrect. A cut in repo rate means cost of borrowing from the Reserve Bank
will be lower for commercial banks. The rate cut will further help banks to lower loan interest
rates for borrowers.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding the ‘Monetary Policy Committee (MPC)? 1. It decides the RBI’s benchmark interest rates. 2. It is a 12-member body including the Governor of RBI and is reconstituted every year. 3. It functions under the chairmanship of the Union Finance Minister. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Option a is the correct answer
Statement 1 is correct. The Monetary Policy Committee is a statutory and institutionalized
framework under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934, for maintaining price stability, while keeping
in mind the objective of growth.
Statements 2 and 3 are incorrect. The Governor of RBI is ex-officio Chairman of the committee.
The committee comprises six members (including the Chairman) – three officials of the RBI and
three external members nominated by the Government of India.

subject: Economy
question: When the Reserve Bank of India reduces the Statutory Liquidity Ratio by 50 basis points, which of the following is likely to happen?

options.a: India’s GDP growth rate increases drastically
options.b: Foreign Institutional Investors may bring more capital into our country
options.c: Scheduled Commercial Banks may cut their lending rates.
options.d: It may drastically reduce the liquidity to the banking system

explanation:
Option c is the correct answer
